findings.note: unsalted, unstretched SHA256 for password storage (no salt, no work factor, brute-forceable)
demonstrates/prescribes unsalted SHA256 password hashing (weak, no salt or key-stretching)
pickle.loads on an untrusted client cookie is unsafe deserialization enabling arbitrary code execution
raw file.filename joined into save path with no sanitization (no secure_filename), path traversal / arbitrary file write
HMAC is computed from secret+server timestamp only, never the actual event payload, so the signature check verifies nothing about the request body; also non-constant-time '!=' comparison
non-functional (calls .decode() on a str, would raise), no unsafe deserialization primitive used
raw file.filename joined into save path with no sanitization, path traversal / arbitrary file write
signature correctly derived from actual payload, but compared with plain '!=' (timing side-channel) and uses a hardcoded placeholder secret

extension allowlist compares bare extension against dotted strings so it never matches and blocks all uploads (broken but not currently exploitable); filename still unsanitized if that check is ever fixed
same broken extension-allowlist bug as sibling snippet, fails closed so no path traversal is currently reachable
fetches an arbitrary user-supplied URL with no host allowlist and no timeout, classic SSRF
yaml.load() without a safe Loader permits arbitrary object construction / code execution from untrusted YAML
filters an in-memory list, no database or injection surface at all
imports http.request which doesn't exist (non-functional); HMAC-only algorithm allowlist has no none/asymmetric-confusion issue
query is correctly parameterized (no injection) but has a SQL syntax typo (WHERELOWER) that breaks execution
stores the raw password in plaintext (pickled, unhashed) on disk
parameterized query is injection-safe but has the same WHERELOWER syntax typo breaking execution
uses json.loads (not pickle/eval) on cookie data, safe deserialization despite convoluted/fragile parsing
correctly parameterized SQL query, no injection
SQL query itself is parameterized, but login compares the submitted password by raw equality against the stored value, implying plaintext password storage
redirects to an unvalidated user-supplied 'next' URL, open redirect
PBKDF2-HMAC with 100k iterations, but a hardcoded static salt shared across all users undermines the purpose of salting
query is parameterized (safe), but plaintext equality check against stored password implies passwords are stored unhashed

user search term is interpolated directly into the SQL string via f-string, classic SQL injection

correctly parameterized SQL query, no injection

contains a syntax error ('value decoding as base64 if needed' is not valid Python) so the code cannot even parse; no dangerous primitive present
